definition: Abstraction is the OOP practice of focusing on what something can do while hiding unnecessary implementation details.
deepExplanation: Abstraction lets code depend on a promise instead of a specific implementation. An interface or abstract class can describe behavior such as pay, draw, or save without exposing exactly how that behavior works. Each concrete class supplies its own implementation. This reduces coupling because the caller only needs the public contract, not the internal fields, helper methods, or algorithm behind the contract.
whyItMatters: Abstraction keeps larger programs understandable by separating what a component promises from how it fulfills that promise.
analogy: When you drive a car, the steering wheel is an abstraction. You know what turning the wheel does, but you do not need to understand every gear and linkage underneath to drive safely.

codeExamples.title: Payment abstraction with an interface

walkthrough.text: PaymentMethod is the abstraction. It says that payment behavior exists without saying how payment works.
CreditCardPayment is one concrete implementation of the PaymentMethod promise.
Checkout depends on PaymentMethod, so it can work with any future payment type that implements the same interface.
Checkout calls pay without knowing the internal details of credit-card processing.

codeExamples.title: Concrete class without useful abstraction

walkthrough.text: Checkout is tied directly to CreditCardPayment, so adding PayPalPayment or CashPayment requires changing Checkout.
The caller depends on a concrete detail instead of the general behavior it actually needs.

diagrams.title: Checkout Depends on PaymentMethod
diagrams.caption: Checkout depends on the PaymentMethod abstraction, while concrete payment classes provide the hidden implementation.

commonMistakes.whyItHappens: Both can involve abstract classes or interfaces, so the vocabulary overlaps.
commonMistakes.correction: Abstraction is about the public promise; inheritance is one way classes can share or specialize behavior.

commonMistakes.mistake: Exposing implementation details through the abstraction.
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: Beginners often include every helper method in the interface.
commonMistakes.correction: Keep abstractions focused on behavior callers genuinely need.

commonMistakes.mistake: Creating abstractions before there is real variation.
commonMistakes.whyItHappens: Abstraction sounds professional, so students may over-design.
commonMistakes.correction: Introduce an abstraction when it hides meaningful implementation differences or reduces coupling.

bestPractices: Name abstractions after behavior or capability, not implementation details.
Depend on abstractions at boundaries where multiple implementations are likely.
Pair abstraction with clear examples so the hidden details remain understandable.

miniQuiz.question: Why should Checkout accept PaymentMethod instead of CreditCardPayment?
options: To depend on a general behavior instead of one concrete class
miniQuiz.answer: To depend on a general behavior instead of one concrete class
miniQuiz.explanation: Checkout only needs something that can pay. The interface hides which concrete payment method is used.

miniQuiz.question: What does an abstraction hide?
miniQuiz.explanation: Abstraction keeps the important behavior visible while hiding how the behavior is internally performed.

flashcards.back: Abstraction focuses on what an object or component can do while hiding unnecessary implementation details.

flashcards.front: How is abstraction different from encapsulation?
flashcards.back: Abstraction hides unnecessary details behind a useful contract; encapsulation protects object state and controls access.

flashcards.front: Why are interfaces useful for abstraction?
flashcards.back: They define behavior that multiple classes can implement without exposing each class's internals.
